title: AWS, Azure, GCP, Heroku, And Snowflake
objective: Build a fast, memorable comparison of the major platforms most non-technical learners are likely to encounter.
beginnerExplanation: AWS is the broadest general cloud platform and is often the default reference point because it offers a huge range of infrastructure and managed services. Azure is Microsoft's enterprise-heavy cloud and often fits organisations already invested in Microsoft tooling, identity, and procurement. GCP is Google's cloud platform and is especially strong in modern infrastructure, data, and machine learning workflows. Heroku is a simpler platform designed to make application deployment easier and more approachable. Snowflake is different again: it is not a full general cloud provider, but a cloud-native data platform built for warehousing, analytics, sharing, and governed data work.
deeperExplanation:
For a non-technical learner, the goal is not to memorise product catalogs. It is to understand each platform's center of gravity.

AWS is broad and deep, which makes it powerful but potentially overwhelming. Azure often wins where enterprise identity, Microsoft contracts, and integrated corporate IT matter. GCP often feels attractive where analytics, data engineering, and modern developer workflows are central. Heroku is valuable because it demonstrates the power of simplicity and fast developer onboarding. Snowflake matters because many companies are not really choosing a general cloud first; they are choosing how to organise and activate data across teams.

The practical lesson is that cloud choices are usually about fit, not abstract superiority. The right question is not which platform is best in theory, but which platform best matches the team's skills, current ecosystem, data needs, governance needs, and desired speed.
keyTakeaways: AWS is the broadest platform, Azure the enterprise-heavy one, GCP the data-and-ML-friendly one
Heroku optimises for simplicity and speed
Snowflake is a data cloud, not a general infrastructure cloud
Platform choice is mostly about fit, not brand mythology
example: If someone at CrowdStrike hears a sales engineer talk about EC2, EKS, S3, and Snowflake, the simplest interpretation is: EC2 means rented virtual machines, EKS means Kubernetes-managed application workloads, S3 means storage for files or logs, and Snowflake means the analytics and governed data layer rather than general infrastructure.
commonMistakes: Picking a platform based only on popularity
Treating Snowflake like a full replacement for general cloud infrastructure
Ignoring the team's existing tools, skills, and procurement reality
exercise: Choose one imaginary company and explain which of the five platforms would most likely be the primary fit and why.

mentalModel.title: Platform fit before feature lists
mentalModel.summary: Start with the provider's center of gravity and the team's context before comparing long service catalogs.

howItWorks.title: Comparison process
steps: Define the main business job the platform needs to support
Check the team's current ecosystem and strengths
Identify whether the priority is breadth, enterprise integration, simplicity, or data capability
Choose the provider whose shape matches the actual need

upsides: Makes vendor comparison easier for non-specialists
downsides: High-level summaries cannot capture every edge case
Some organisations use several of these platforms together
teaching.workedExample: A CrowdStrike-style AWS conversation might sound more complex than it is. The non-technical map is: customers use an app, the app runs on cloud services, some workloads may sit on EC2 virtual machines, some may be orchestrated through Kubernetes on EKS, files and telemetry often land in S3, and analytics may flow into a platform like Snowflake.
whoWorksWithThis: Founders, product leaders, and operations managers

goDeeper.label: See Cloud cost, risk, and governance
